chronology.title: Henri de Tonti reaches a Natchitoches village (1690)
chronology.description: Source marks Henri de Tonti reaching a Natchitoches village while searching for La Salle’s lost expedition.

chronology.title: Natchitoches absorption of Ouachita (c. 1690)
chronology.description: Source indicates the Natchitoches had absorbed the Ouachita by around 1690, signaling dynamic pre-contact political change.

chronology.title: St. Denis establishes French post at old Natchitoches village (1714)
chronology.description: Source states St. Denis established the French post in 1714 at an old Natchitoches village site.

coreTruthSet.description: The Natchitoches are presented as part of a broader Caddoan civilizational network rather than an isolated tribal unit.
coreTruthSet.quoteExcerpt: The Natchitoches were part of the broader Caddoan world, one branch of a large network of related peoples...

coreTruthSet.description: Matrilineal descent and women’s authority in fields and household spaces are treated as structural social facts.
coreTruthSet.quoteExcerpt: Across the Caddo world, descent was traditionally matrilineal... elder women controlled... lodges... and fields.

coreTruthSet.description: The source frames Natchitoches life as riverine, agricultural, and hamlet-based rather than nomadic.
coreTruthSet.quoteExcerpt: Like other Caddoan peoples, the Natchitoches belonged to an agricultural society... a series of small hamlets...

coreTruthSet.description: The area later used for the French post is described as pre-existing Indigenous infrastructure in a trade geography.
coreTruthSet.quoteExcerpt: They built there because the place was already native infrastructure: a node in an older trade geography.

settingAnchors.name: Natchitoches Area (historic Indigenous node)
settingAnchors.description: Regional node described as a long-standing Indigenous trade and settlement center before colonial attachment.

settingAnchors.name: Red River Valley (Natchitoches homeland corridor)
settingAnchors.description: Floodplain, levees, cane breaks, and settlement ground framing agriculture, mobility, and strategic life.

continuityConstraints.title: Ceremony, Place, and Ancestor Order
continuityConstraints.text: Temples, cemeteries, and ceremonial life bind social order to land, ancestors, and seasonal continuity.

continuityConstraints.title: Distributed Hamlet Governance
continuityConstraints.summary: Governance pattern constraint for scene legitimacy.
continuityConstraints.text: Power appears distributed across linked hamlets, kinship, ritual authority, and local leadership rather than a single rigid center.

continuityConstraints.title: River Trade Witness Density
continuityConstraints.text: River exchange nodes function as socially dense theaters where diplomacy, reputation, and risk circulate together.

openResearchGaps.title: Which Natchitoches-specific ritual practices are directly sourced vs inferred from broader Caddo records?
openResearchGaps.description: Clarify direct evidence boundaries before scene-level ritual detail enters the core story bible.

openResearchGaps.title: How exactly did leadership authority vary across Natchitoches hamlets?
openResearchGaps.description: Need tighter differentiation between hereditary offices, consensus councils, and ritual authority in local governance.

openResearchGaps.title: What was the operating diplomatic map among Natchitoches, Adaes, Yatasi, Ouachita, and Hasinai at key dates?
openResearchGaps.description: Needed for conflict architecture and alliance plausibility in chapter planning.
